qanta-buzzer 2: # Adapted from qb-rl structure for T5-based quiz bowl agent 3: 4: data: 5: csv_path: "questions.csv" # Raw QANTA CSV with ||| separated clues 6: K: 4 # Default number of answer choices 7: distractor_strategy: "sbert_profile" # sbert_profile | tfidf_profile | category_random | openai_profile 8: variable_K: false # If true, sample K per question from [min_K, max_K] 9: min_K: 2 10: max_K: null # Defaults to K when null 11: train_ratio: 0.7 12: val_ratio: 0.15 13: test_ratio: 0.15 14: max_questions: null # Limit for testing (null = use all) 15: shuffle_seed: 42 16: 17: answer_profiles: 18: max_tokens_per_profile: 2000 # Max tokens to use for answer profile 19: min_questions_per_answer: 1 # Minimum examples to build profile 20: leave_one_out: true # Exclude current question from profile 21: 22: likelihood: 23: model: "t5-large" # Model for computing answer likelihoods (t5-small | t5-base | t5-large) 24: embedding_model: "all-MiniLM-L6-v2" # For distractor generation 25: beta: 5.0 # Softmax temperature for belief distribution 26: cache_embeddings: true 27: cache_dir: "cache/embeddings" 28: batch_size: 16 29: max_length: 512 # Max input tokens for T5 30: 31: environment: 32: reward_mode: "time_penalty" # time_penalty | simple | expected_wins 33: seed: 13 34: wait_penalty: 0.05 # Tuned candidate from multi-seed smoke sweep 35: early_buzz_penalty: 0.2 # Tuned candidate from multi-seed smoke sweep 36: buzz_correct: 1.0 # Reward for correct answer 37: buzz_incorrect: -0.5 # Penalty for wrong answer 38: max_steps: 20 # Maximum clues to reveal 39: # Expected Wins opponent model (only used when reward_mode: expected_wins) 40: opponent_buzz_model: 41: type: "none" # none | logistic | empirical 42: end_mode: "force_commit" # force_commit | no_buzz 43: no_buzz_reward: 0.0 # Only used when end_mode == no_buzz 44: 45: mc_guards: # Anti-artifact guards from qb-rl 46: alias_edit_distance_threshold: 0.2 # Reject similar answer aliases 47: duplicate_token_overlap_threshold: 0.8 # Reject token-overlapping distractors 48: max_length_ratio: 3.0 # Reject distractors much longer than answer 49: 50: bayesian: # Bayesian buzzer sweep parameters (from qb-rl) 51: threshold_sweep: [0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9] 52: alpha: 10.0 # Sigmoid steepness for confidence proxy 53: 54: ppo: # PPO hyperparameters (for future use) 55: seed: 13 56: total_timesteps: 100000 57: learning_rate: 3e-4 58: n_steps: 128 59: batch_size: 32 60: n_epochs: 4 61: gamma: 0.99 62: gae_lambda: 0.95 63: clip_ratio: 0.2 64: value_coef: 0.5 65: entropy_coef: 0.01 66: max_grad_norm: 0.5 67: target_kl: 0.03 68: policy_kwargs: 69: net_arch: [64, 64] # MLP architecture for belief-based policy 70: 71: evaluation: 72: metrics: 73: - accuracy 74: - reward 75: - buzz_position 76: - calibration # ECE and Brier score 77: - per_category 78: compute_sq: true # S_q scoring metric 79: run_choices_only: true # Control: model sees only choices, no clues 80: run_shuffle: true # Control: shuffle clue order 81: bootstrap_ci_samples: 1000 # Bootstrap confidence intervals 82: save_predictions: true 83: prediction_dir: "results/predictions" 84: 85: # DSPy integration (optional, offline-first) 86: # Activated by setting likelihood.model: dspy — no separate enable flag. 87: dspy: 88: model: "openai/gpt-4o-mini" # DSPy LM identifier 89: optimizer: "BootstrapFewShot" # BootstrapFewShot | MIPROv2 90: cache_dir: "cache/dspy" 91: max_examples: 50 92: 93: # Supervised warm-start settings (for T5 policy) 94: supervised: 95: epochs: 10 96: batch_size: 8 97: gradient_accumulation_steps: 4 # Effective batch = 32 98: learning_rate: 1e-4 99: warmup_steps: 500 100: eval_steps: 100 101: save_steps: 500 102: save_total_limit: 3 103: checkpoint_dir: "checkpoints/supervised" 1: """ 2: Likelihood Model Interface 3: 4: Abstract base class for likelihood models that score answer options against 5: revealed clue text. Concrete implementations (TF-IDF, SBERT, T5) inherit 6: from ``LikelihoodModel`` and implement ``score()`` and ``_embed_batch()``. 7: 8: The ``score()`` method returns **raw similarity scores**, not probabilities. 9: The environment applies softmax with a configurable temperature (beta) to 10: convert scores into a belief distribution. 11: 12: Embedding caching is built into the base class: texts are hashed via SHA-256 13: and cached as float32 numpy arrays, so repeated calls with the same text 14: skip recomputation. 15: 16: Ported from qb-rl reference implementation (models/likelihoods.py lines 1-38). 17: """ 18: 19: from __future__ import annotations 20: 21: import hashlib 22: import os 23: from abc import ABC, abstractmethod 24: from pathlib import Path 25: from typing import Any 26: 27: import numpy as np 28: 29: 30: def _text_key(text: str) -> str: 31: """Compute a SHA-256 hash key for embedding cache lookups. 32: 33: Parameters 34: ---------- 35: text : str 36: Input text to hash. 37: 38: Returns 39: ------- 40: str 41: 64-character hexadecimal SHA-256 digest. 42: 43: Examples 44: -------- 45: >>> key = _text_key("hello world") 46: >>> len(key) 47: 64 48: >>> _text_key("hello world") == _text_key("hello world") 49: True 50: """ 51: return hashlib.sha256(text.encode("utf-8")).hexdigest() 52: 53: 54: def _best_torch_device() -> "torch.device": 55: """Select the best available accelerator: CUDA > MPS > CPU.""" 56: import torch 57: 58: if torch.cuda.is_available(): 59: return torch.device("cuda") 60: if hasattr(torch.backends, "mps") and torch.backends.mps.is_available(): 61: return torch.device("mps") 62: return torch.device("cpu") 63: 64: 65: class LikelihoodModel(ABC): 66: """Abstract base class for likelihood models. 67: 68: Likelihood models score how well each answer option matches a given 69: clue prefix. The environment uses these scores (via softmax) to compute 70: belief distributions over answer options. 71: 72: Subclasses must implement: 73: - ``score(clue_prefix, option_profiles) -> np.ndarray`` 74: - ``_embed_batch(texts) -> np.ndarray`` 75: 76: The base class provides ``embed_and_cache()`` which handles caching of 77: text embeddings via SHA-256 content hashing. 78: 79: Attributes 80: ---------- 81: embedding_cache : dict[str, np.ndarray] 82: Maps SHA-256 text hashes to float32 embedding vectors. 83: """ 84: 85: def __init__(self) -> None: 86: self.embedding_cache: dict[str, np.ndarray] = {} 87: 88: @property 89: def cache_memory_bytes(self) -> int: 90: """Approximate memory used by the embedding cache in bytes.""" 91: return sum(v.nbytes for v in self.embedding_cache.values()) 92: 93: @abstractmethod 94: def score(self, clue_prefix: str, option_profiles: list[str]) -> np.ndarray: 95: """Return raw similarity scores for each answer option. 96: 97: The caller (environment) converts these to probabilities via 98: softmax with a beta temperature parameter. Higher scores indicate 99: stronger match between clue and option. 100: 101: Parameters 102: ---------- 103: clue_prefix : str 104: Clue text revealed so far (concatenation of clues up to current step). 105: option_profiles : list[str] 106: Answer profile text for each of the K answer options. 107: 108: Returns 109: ------- 110: np.ndarray 111: Raw similarity scores of shape (K,) where K = len(option_profiles). 112: """ 113: 114: def embed_and_cache(self, texts: list[str]) -> np.ndarray: 115: """Embed texts, using cache for previously seen inputs. 116: 117: Texts are identified by their SHA-256 hash. Only unseen texts 118: are passed to ``_embed_batch()`` for actual computation; cached 119: results are reused. 120: 121: Parameters 122: ---------- 123: texts : list[str] 124: Texts to embed. 125: 126: Returns 127: ------- 128: np.ndarray 129: Stacked embeddings of shape (len(texts), embed_dim), dtype float32. 130: """ 131: missing = [text for text in texts if _text_key(text) not in self.embedding_cache] 132: if missing: 133: new_embeddings = self._embed_batch(missing) 134: for text, emb in zip(missing, new_embeddings): 135: self.embedding_cache[_text_key(text)] = emb.astype(np.float32) 136: return np.stack([self.embedding_cache[_text_key(text)] for text in texts]) 137: 138: def precompute_embeddings( 139: self, 140: texts: list[str], 141: batch_size: int = 64, 142: desc: str = "Pre-computing embeddings", 143: ) -> None: 144: """Bulk pre-embed texts into cache, processing in batches. 145: 146: Call this before running agents so that all subsequent ``score()`` 147: calls are pure cache lookups (numpy dot products). Duplicate and 148: already-cached texts are skipped automatically. 149: 150: Parameters 151: ---------- 152: texts : list[str] 153: All texts to embed (clue prefixes, option profiles, fragments). 154: batch_size : int 155: Number of texts per ``_embed_batch`` call. 156: desc : str 157: tqdm progress-bar description. 158: """ 159: from tqdm import tqdm 160: 161: unique = [t for t in dict.fromkeys(texts) if _text_key(t) not in self.embedding_cache] 162: if not unique: 163: return 164: for i in tqdm(range(0, len(unique), batch_size), desc=desc, 165: total=(len(unique) + batch_size - 1) // batch_size): 166: batch = unique[i : i + batch_size] 167: embeddings = self._embed_batch(batch) 168: for text, emb in zip(batch, embeddings): 169: self.embedding_cache[_text_key(text)] = emb.astype(np.float32) 170: 171: def save_cache(self, path: str | Path) -> int: 172: """Persist embedding_cache to disk as compressed ``.npz``. 173: 174: Creates parent directories if needed. Keys are SHA-256 hex 175: strings (valid Python identifiers), values are float32 arrays. 176: 177: Parameters 178: ---------- 179: path : str or Path 180: Destination file path (should end with ``.npz``). 181: 182: Returns 183: ------- 184: int 185: Number of cache entries saved. 186: """ 187: p = Path(path) 188: p.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) 189: np.savez_compressed(p, **self.embedding_cache) 190: return len(self.embedding_cache) 191: 192: def load_cache(self, path: str | Path) -> int: 193: """Load embedding_cache from a ``.npz`` file on disk. 194: 195: Merges loaded entries into the existing cache **without** 196: overwriting keys that are already present (existing keys win). 197: If the file does not exist, silently returns 0 (cold-start). 198: 199: Parameters 200: ---------- 201: path : str or Path 202: Path to ``.npz`` file previously written by ``save_cache``. 203: 204: Returns 205: ------- 206: int 207: Number of *new* entries added to the cache. 208: """ 209: p = Path(path) 210: if not p.exists(): 211: return 0 212: with np.load(p) as data: 213: loaded = 0 214: for key in data.files: 215: if key not in self.embedding_cache: 216: self.embedding_cache[key] = data[key].astype(np.float32) 217: loaded += 1 218: return loaded 219: 220: @abstractmethod 221: def _embed_batch(self, texts: list[str]) -> np.ndarray: 222: """Embed a batch of texts. Subclasses must implement. 223: 224: Parameters 225: ---------- 226: texts : list[str] 227: Texts to embed (guaranteed non-empty, all cache misses). 228: 229: Returns 230: ------- 231: np.ndarray 232: Embeddings of shape (len(texts), embed_dim), dtype float32. 233: """ 234: raise NotImplementedError 235: 236: 237: class TfIdfLikelihood(LikelihoodModel): 238: """TF-IDF based likelihood model using cosine similarity. 239: 240: Uses scikit-learn's ``TfidfVectorizer`` to learn vocabulary and IDF weights 241: from a corpus, then scores clue-option similarity via cosine distance in the 242: TF-IDF vector space. 243: 244: The model **must** be ``fit()`` on a corpus before calling ``score()`` or 245: ``_embed_batch()``. Calling these methods on an unfitted model raises 246: ``RuntimeError``. 247: 248: This is the fast, interpretable baseline: keyword overlap drives similarity. 249: It works well when clues contain distinctive vocabulary but misses semantic 250: relationships (e.g., "first president" vs "George Washington"). 251: 252: Parameters 253: ---------- 254: corpus_texts : list[str] or None 255: If provided, ``fit()`` is called immediately on these texts. 256: 257: Attributes 258: ---------- 259: vectorizer : TfidfVectorizer 260: Scikit-learn vectorizer with English stop words removed. 261: _is_fit : bool 262: Whether the vectorizer has been fit on a corpus. 263: 264: Examples 265: -------- 266: >>> corpus = ["George Washington was the first president", 267: ... "Abraham Lincoln freed the slaves"] 268: >>> model = TfIdfLikelihood(corpus_texts=corpus) 269: >>> scores = model.score("first president", ["Washington", "Lincoln"]) 270: >>> scores.shape 271: (2,) 272: """ 273: 274: def __init__(self, corpus_texts: list[str] | None = None) -> None: 275: super().__init__() 276: from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer 277: 278: self.vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer(stop_words="english") 279: self._is_fit = False 280: if corpus_texts: 281: self.fit(corpus_texts) 282: 283: def save_cache(self, path: str | Path) -> int: 284: """No-op: TF-IDF embeddings are vocabulary-specific and not portable. 285: 286: TF-IDF vectors depend on the fitted vocabulary, which changes 287: between ``fit()`` calls. Persisting them would produce wrong 288: results if the vocabulary differs. 289: 290: Returns 291: ------- 292: int 293: Always 0. 294: """ 295: return 0 296: 297: def fit(self, corpus_texts: list[str]) -> "TfIdfLikelihood": 298: """Learn vocabulary and IDF weights from a text corpus. 299: 300: Parameters 301: ---------- 302: corpus_texts : list[str] 303: Corpus of documents to learn from. Should include answer profiles, 304: clue texts, or both to capture domain vocabulary. 305: 306: Returns 307: ------- 308: TfIdfLikelihood 309: Self, for method chaining. 310: """ 311: self.vectorizer.fit(corpus_texts) 312: self._is_fit = True 313: return self 314: 315: def score(self, clue_prefix: str, option_profiles: list[str]) -> np.ndarray: 316: """Score each option against the clue using TF-IDF cosine similarity. 317: 318: Uses ``embed_and_cache()`` to embed both the clue and options, so 319: repeated calls with the same texts skip vectorizer.transform(). 320: Since ``_embed_batch()`` returns L2-normalized vectors, the dot 321: product equals cosine similarity. 322: 323: Parameters 324: ---------- 325: clue_prefix : str 326: Clue text revealed so far. 327: option_profiles : list[str] 328: Answer profile text for each of the K answer options. 329: 330: Returns 331: ------- 332: np.ndarray 333: Cosine similarity scores of shape (K,), dtype float32. 334: Values in [-1, 1] but typically [0, 1] for TF-IDF. 335: 336: Raises 337: ------ 338: RuntimeError 339: If called before ``fit()``. 340: """ 341: if not self._is_fit: 342: raise RuntimeError("TfIdfLikelihood must be fit() before score().") 343: clue_emb = self.embed_and_cache([clue_prefix])[0] 344: option_embs = self.embed_and_cache(option_profiles) 345: sims = option_embs @ clue_emb 346: return sims.astype(np.float32) 347: 348: def _embed_batch(self, texts: list[str]) -> np.ndarray: 349: """Embed texts as dense, L2-normalized TF-IDF vectors. 350: 351: Row-wise L2 normalization ensures that dot product between any 352: two embedding vectors equals their cosine similarity, matching 353: the convention used by SBERT and T5 likelihood models. 354: 355: Parameters 356: ---------- 357: texts : list[str] 358: Texts to embed (guaranteed non-empty, all cache misses). 359: 360: Returns 361: ------- 362: np.ndarray 363: L2-normalized dense TF-IDF matrix of shape 364: (len(texts), vocab_size), dtype float32. 365: 366: Raises 367: ------ 368: RuntimeError 369: If called before ``fit()``. 370: """ 371: if not self._is_fit: 372: raise RuntimeError("TfIdfLikelihood must be fit() before embedding.") 373: mat = self.vectorizer.transform(texts).toarray().astype(np.float32) 374: norms = np.linalg.norm(mat, axis=1, keepdims=True) 375: norms[norms == 0] = 1.0 # avoid division by zero for empty docs 376: return mat / norms 377: 378: 379: class SBERTLikelihood(LikelihoodModel): 380: """Sentence-BERT likelihood model using semantic embeddings. 381: 382: Uses a ``SentenceTransformer`` model to compute dense, L2-normalized 383: embeddings. Cosine similarity is computed as a simple dot product since 384: embeddings are pre-normalized (``normalize_embeddings=True``). 385: 386: Inherits ``embed_and_cache()`` from ``LikelihoodModel`` for transparent 387: caching of embeddings via SHA-256 content hashing. The first call to 388: ``score()`` computes and caches all embeddings; subsequent calls with the 389: same texts are fast cache lookups. 390: 391: Compared to TF-IDF, SBERT captures semantic similarity (e.g., "first 392: president" and "George Washington" score highly even without word overlap) 393: but is slower due to the neural encoder. 394: 395: Parameters 396: ---------- 397: model_name : str 398: HuggingFace model identifier for ``SentenceTransformer``. 399: Default is ``"all-MiniLM-L6-v2"`` (22M params, 384-dim embeddings). 400: First run downloads the model (~80MB) from HuggingFace. 401: 402: Attributes 403: ---------- 404: model_name : str 405: The SentenceTransformer model name. 406: encoder : SentenceTransformer 407: The loaded sentence transformer model. 408: 409: Examples 410: -------- 411: >>> model = SBERTLikelihood() # downloads model on first run 412: >>> scores = model.score("first president", ["Washington", "Lincoln"]) 413: >>> scores.shape 414: (2,) 415: """ 416: 417: def __init__(self, model_name: str = "all-MiniLM-L6-v2") -> None: 418: super().__init__() 419: from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer 420: 421: self.model_name = model_name 422: self.encoder = SentenceTransformer(model_name) 423: 424: def _embed_batch(self, texts: list[str]) -> np.ndarray: 425: """Embed texts using the SentenceTransformer encoder. 426: 427: Embeddings are L2-normalized so that cosine similarity can be computed 428: as a simple dot product (avoiding the division by norms). 429: 430: Parameters 431: ---------- 432: texts : list[str] 433: Texts to embed (guaranteed non-empty, all cache misses). 434: 435: Returns 436: ------- 437: np.ndarray 438: Normalized embeddings of shape (len(texts), embed_dim), dtype float32. 439: """ 440: return self.encoder.encode( 441: texts, convert_to_numpy=True, normalize_embeddings=True 442: ).astype(np.float32) 443: 444: def score(self, clue_prefix: str, option_profiles: list[str]) -> np.ndarray: 445: """Score each option using semantic cosine similarity. 446: 447: Computes dot product between the clue embedding and each option 448: embedding. Since embeddings are L2-normalized, dot product equals 449: cosine similarity. 450: 451: Parameters 452: ---------- 453: clue_prefix : str 454: Clue text revealed so far. 455: option_profiles : list[str] 456: Answer profile text for each of the K answer options. 457: 458: Returns 459: ------- 460: np.ndarray 461: Cosine similarity scores of shape (K,), dtype float32. 462: Values in [-1, 1]. 463: """ 464: clue_emb = self.embed_and_cache([clue_prefix])[0] 465: option_embs = self.embed_and_cache(option_profiles) 466: sims = option_embs @ clue_emb 467: return sims.astype(np.float32) 468: 469: 470: class OpenAILikelihood(LikelihoodModel): 471: """OpenAI embedding likelihood model using normalized embedding similarity. 472: 473: This path is optional and only activates when explicitly selected in config. 474: It requires both the ``openai`` Python package and ``OPENAI_API_KEY`` to be 475: available at runtime. 476: """ 477: 478: def __init__( 479: self, 480: model: str = "text-embedding-3-small", 481: api_key: str | None = None, 482: ) -> None: 483: super().__init__() 484: 485: resolved_api_key = api_key or os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY") 486: if not resolved_api_key: 487: raise RuntimeError( 488: "OpenAI likelihood requires OPENAI_API_KEY to be set." 489: ) 490: 491: try: 492: from openai import OpenAI 493: except ImportError as exc: 494: raise ImportError( 495: "OpenAI likelihood requires the openai package. " 496: "Install it with: pip install -e .[openai] or pip install openai." 497: ) from exc 498: 499: self.model = model 500: self.client = OpenAI(api_key=resolved_api_key) 501: 502: def _embed_batch(self, texts: list[str]) -> np.ndarray: 503: """Embed texts via the OpenAI embeddings API and L2-normalize them.""" 504: response = self.client.embeddings.create(model=self.model, input=texts) 505: vectors = [np.array(item.embedding, dtype=np.float32) for item in response.data] 506: embeddings = np.stack(vectors) 507: norms = np.linalg.norm(embeddings, axis=1, keepdims=True) 508: norms[norms == 0] = 1.0 509: return (embeddings / norms).astype(np.float32) 510: 511: def score(self, clue_prefix: str, option_profiles: list[str]) -> np.ndarray: 512: """Score each option using cosine similarity over normalized embeddings.""" 513: clue_emb = self.embed_and_cache([clue_prefix])[0] 514: option_embs = self.embed_and_cache(option_profiles) 515: sims = option_embs @ clue_emb 516: return sims.astype(np.float32) 517: 518: 519: class T5Likelihood(LikelihoodModel): 520: """T5 encoder likelihood model using mean-pooled semantic embeddings. 521: 522: Uses ``T5EncoderModel`` (not full ``T5ForConditionalGeneration``) for 2x 523: faster inference and half the memory. Embeddings are mean-pooled over 524: sequence length with attention mask weighting to handle padding correctly. 525: 526: Inherits ``embed_and_cache()`` from ``LikelihoodModel`` for transparent 527: caching of embeddings via SHA-256 content hashing. The first call to 528: ``score()`` computes and caches all embeddings; subsequent calls with the 529: same texts are fast cache lookups. 530: 531: Compared to SBERT, T5 captures deeper semantic relationships via its 532: encoder-decoder pre-training on massive text corpora. This is the novel 533: contribution: using T5 as a likelihood model rather than just as a policy 534: encoder. 535: 536: Parameters 537: ---------- 538: model_name : str 539: HuggingFace T5 model identifier. Default is ``"t5-base"`` 540: (220M params). Options: 541: 542: - ``"t5-small"`` (60M params) -- fastest, lowest quality 543: - ``"t5-base"`` (220M params) -- balanced (recommended) 544: - ``"t5-large"`` (770M params) -- best quality, requires 8GB GPU VRAM 545: 546: First run downloads the model from HuggingFace (~850MB for t5-base). 547: 548: Attributes 549: ---------- 550: model_name : str 551: The T5 model identifier. 552: encoder : T5EncoderModel 553: Pre-trained T5 encoder loaded from HuggingFace. 554: tokenizer : T5TokenizerFast 555: Fast T5 tokenizer for text preprocessing. 556: device : torch.device 557: Computation device (cuda if available, else cpu). 558: 559: Examples 560: -------- 561: >>> model = T5Likelihood(model_name="t5-small") 562: >>> scores = model.score("first president", ["Washington", "Einstein"]) 563: >>> scores.shape 564: (2,) 565: """ 566: 567: def __init__(self, model_name: str = "t5-base") -> None: 568: super().__init__() 569: import torch 570: from transformers import T5EncoderModel, T5TokenizerFast 571: 572: self.model_name = model_name 573: self.encoder = T5EncoderModel.from_pretrained(model_name) 574: self.tokenizer = T5TokenizerFast.from_pretrained(model_name) 575: self.device = _best_torch_device() 576: self.encoder.to(self.device) 577: self.encoder.eval() 578: 579: def _embed_batch(self, texts: list[str]) -> np.ndarray: 580: """Embed texts using T5 encoder with attention-masked mean pooling. 581: 582: Mean pooling uses the attention mask to exclude padding tokens from the 583: average, ensuring correct semantic embeddings when sequences have 584: different lengths. Embeddings are L2-normalized so that cosine 585: similarity can be computed as a simple dot product. 586: 587: Parameters 588: ---------- 589: texts : list[str] 590: Texts to embed (guaranteed non-empty, all cache misses). 591: 592: Returns 593: ------- 594: np.ndarray 595: L2-normalized embeddings of shape (len(texts), hidden_dim), 596: dtype float32. Hidden dim is 512 (t5-small), 768 (t5-base), 597: or 1024 (t5-large). 598: 599: Notes 600: ----- 601: Tensors are detached and moved to CPU immediately after computation 602: to prevent GPU memory leaks when called repeatedly during episodes. 603: """ 604: import torch 605: 606: with torch.no_grad(): 607: encoded = self.tokenizer( 608: texts, 609: padding=True, 610: truncation=True, 611: max_length=512, 612: return_tensors="pt", 613: ).to(self.device) 614: 615: outputs = self.encoder(**encoded) 616: last_hidden = outputs.last_hidden_state # (batch, seq_len, hidden_dim) 617: 618: # Mean pooling over sequence length with attention mask 619: mask = encoded.attention_mask.unsqueeze(-1) # (batch, seq_len, 1) 620: masked_hidden = last_hidden * mask 621: sum_hidden = masked_hidden.sum(dim=1) # (batch, hidden_dim) 622: mask_sum = mask.sum(dim=1).clamp(min=1e-9) # (batch, 1) 623: mean_pooled = sum_hidden / mask_sum # (batch, hidden_dim) 624: 625: # L2 normalize for cosine similarity via dot product 626: embeddings = torch.nn.functional.normalize(mean_pooled, p=2, dim=1) 627: 628: # Detach and move to CPU to prevent GPU memory leak 629: embeddings = embeddings.detach().cpu().numpy().astype(np.float32) 630: 631: return embeddings 632: 633: def score(self, clue_prefix: str, option_profiles: list[str]) -> np.ndarray: 634: """Score each option using T5 semantic cosine similarity. 635: 636: Computes dot product between the clue embedding and each option 637: embedding. Since embeddings are L2-normalized, dot product equals 638: cosine similarity. 639: 640: Parameters 641: ---------- 642: clue_prefix : str 643: Clue text revealed so far. 644: option_profiles : list[str] 645: Answer profile text for each of the K answer options. 646: 647: Returns 648: ------- 649: np.ndarray 650: Cosine similarity scores of shape (K,), dtype float32. 651: Values in [-1, 1]. 652: """ 653: clue_emb = self.embed_and_cache([clue_prefix])[0] 654: option_embs = self.embed_and_cache(option_profiles) 655: sims = option_embs @ clue_emb 656: return sims.astype(np.float32) 657: 658: 659: def build_likelihood_from_config( 660: config: dict[str, Any], corpus_texts: list[str] | None = None 661: ) -> LikelihoodModel: 662: """Construct a likelihood model from YAML configuration. 663: 664: Factory function that reads the ``likelihood`` section of the config dict 665: and instantiates the appropriate ``LikelihoodModel`` subclass. 666: 667: Parameters 668: ---------- 669: config : dict[str, Any] 670: Full YAML config dict. Must contain a ``"likelihood"`` key with at 671: least a ``"model"`` field specifying the model type. 672: 673: Supported model types: 674: - ``"tfidf"``: TF-IDF cosine similarity (requires ``corpus_texts``) 675: - ``"sbert"``: Sentence-BERT semantic similarity 676: - ``"openai"``: OpenAI embedding similarity 677: - ``"t5"`` / ``"t5-small"`` / ``"t5-base"`` / ``"t5-large"``: 678: T5 encoder semantic similarity 679: 680: Optional config keys: 681: - ``"sbert_name"`` or ``"embedding_model"``: SentenceTransformer model 682: name (default: ``"all-MiniLM-L6-v2"``) 683: - ``"openai_model"``: OpenAI embedding model name 684: (default: ``"text-embedding-3-small"``) 685: - ``"t5_name"``: T5 model name (default: ``"t5-base"``) 686: 687: corpus_texts : list[str] or None 688: Text corpus for TF-IDF fitting. Required when ``model == "tfidf"``, 689: ignored for other models. 690: 691: Returns 692: ------- 693: LikelihoodModel 694: An instantiated and ready-to-use likelihood model. 695: 696: Raises 697: ------ 698: ValueError 699: If ``model`` is ``"tfidf"`` and ``corpus_texts`` is None. 700: If ``model`` is not a recognized model type. 701: 702: Examples 703: -------- 704: >>> from qb_data.config import load_config 705: >>> config = load_config("configs/default.yaml") 706: >>> model = build_likelihood_from_config(config, corpus_texts=my_corpus) 707: >>> scores = model.score("first president", ["Washington", "Lincoln"]) 708: """ 709: cfg = config["likelihood"] 710: model_name = cfg.get("model", "sbert") 711: 712: if model_name == "tfidf": 713: if not corpus_texts: 714: raise ValueError("TF-IDF likelihood requires corpus_texts.") 715: return TfIdfLikelihood(corpus_texts=corpus_texts) 716: 717: if model_name == "sbert": 718: # Support both "sbert_name" (qb-rl convention) and 719: # "embedding_model" (qanta-buzzer default.yaml convention) 720: sbert_name = cfg.get("sbert_name", cfg.get("embedding_model", "all-MiniLM-L6-v2")) 721: return SBERTLikelihood(model_name=sbert_name) 722: 723: if model_name == "openai": 724: return OpenAILikelihood( 725: model=cfg.get("openai_model", "text-embedding-3-small"), 726: ) 727: 728: if model_name == "t5": 729: t5_name = cfg.get("t5_name", "t5-base") 730: return T5Likelihood(model_name=t5_name) 731: 732: if isinstance(model_name, str) and model_name.startswith("t5"): 733: t5_name = model_name 734: return T5Likelihood(model_name=t5_name) 735: 736: if model_name == "dspy": 737: try: 738: from models.dspy_likelihood import DSPyLikelihood 739: except ImportError as exc: 740: raise ImportError( 741: "DSPy likelihood requires the dspy package. " 742: "Install with: pip install -e '.[dspy]'" 743: ) from exc 744: dspy_cfg = config.get("dspy", {}) 745: cache_dir = dspy_cfg.get("cache_dir") 746: fingerprint = dspy_cfg.get("program_fingerprint", "default") 747: 748: def _placeholder_scorer(clue: str, options: list[str]) -> list[float]: 749: return [1.0 / max(1, len(options))] * len(options) 750: 751: return DSPyLikelihood( 752: scorer=_placeholder_scorer, 753: program_fingerprint=fingerprint, 754: cache_dir=cache_dir, 755: ) 756: 757: raise ValueError(f"Unknown likelihood model: {model_name}") 1: # AGENTS.md 2: 3: Canonical repo contract for all coding agents (Claude, Copilot, Cursor, etc.). 4: 5: ## Project Overview 6: 7: Stanford CS234 final project: a unified quiz bowl RL buzzer system with two tracks. The belief-feature pipeline builds MC tossups, scores answer profiles with TF-IDF / SBERT / T5 / optional OpenAI / optional DSPy, trains or compares buzzers, and evaluates with S_q, Expected Wins, and calibration metrics. The T5 policy pipeline provides supervised warm-start and PPO for an end-to-end text policy using factorized stop/answer semantics (`P(WAIT)` and `P(BUZZ_i) = P(BUZZ) * P(answer_i | BUZZ)`). Three opt-in extensions: Expected Wins reward mode, variable-K answer choices, and DSPy integration. Additional opt-in feature-port surfaces are available for stop-only PPO (`scripts/train_ppo.py --policy-mode stop_only`) and no-buzz horizon behavior (`environment.end_mode: no_buzz`). `qanta-buzzer` is the canonical repo. qb-rl compatibility is preserved through additive shims rather than structural rewrites. 8: 9: ## Setup 10: 11: Requires Python >= 3.11. 12: 13: ```bash 14: python3 -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate 15: pip install -U pip && pip install -e . 16: ``` 17: 18: Optional extras: 19: 20: ```bash 21: pip install -e '.[openai]' # OpenAI embedding support 22: pip install -e '.[maskable]' # MaskablePPO for variable-K 23: pip install -e '.[dspy]' # DSPy LM-based scoring 24: ``` 25: 26: ## Architecture 27: 28: | Package | Purpose | 29: |---------|---------| 30: | `qb_data/` | Data loading, answer profiles, stratified splits, MC construction, DSPy profiles | 31: | `qb_env/` | Gymnasium environment, text wrapper, opponent models, optional StopOnlyEnv wrapper, qb-rl shims | 32: | `models/` | Likelihood models (TF-IDF, SBERT, T5, OpenAI, DSPy), belief features, T5 policy | 33: | `agents/` | Threshold, softmax-profile, sequential Bayes, PPO wrapper | 34: | `evaluation/` | S_q metric, Expected Wins, calibration, control experiments, plotting | 35: | `scripts/` | Pipeline entrypoints, DSPy compile, shared helpers | 36: | `training/` | T5 policy supervised + PPO trainers, hazard bridge utilities | 37: | `configs/` | YAML configuration files (default, smoke, t5_policy) | 38: 39: ## Testing 40: 41: 342 tests across 24 test files (3 skipped when optional extras not installed). 42: 43: ```bash 44: pytest # full suite 45: pytest tests/test_qb_rl_bridge.py tests/test_factories.py tests/test_ppo_buzzer.py # focused bridge/runtime checks 46: scripts/ci.sh # CI entry point (runs pytest, exits nonzero on failure) 47: ``` 48: 49: ## Smoke Pipeline 50: 51: Four-stage belief-feature smoke workflow. `--smoke` selects `configs/smoke.yaml` and writes outputs to `artifacts/smoke/`. 52: 53: ```bash 54: python scripts/build_mc_dataset.py --smoke 55: python scripts/run_baselines.py --smoke 56: python scripts/train_ppo.py --smoke 57: python scripts/evaluate_all.py --smoke 58: ``` 59: 60: Or run all four stages via the wrapper script: 61: 62: ```bash 63: scripts/manual-smoke.sh 64: ``` 65: 66: ## T5 Policy Pipeline 67: 68: ```bash 69: python scripts/train_t5_policy.py --config configs/t5_policy.yaml 70: python scripts/compare_policies.py --config configs/t5_policy.yaml 71: ``` 72: 73: Notes: 74: `scripts/train_t5_policy.py` parses `--hazard-pretrain`, `--beta-terminal`, and `--freeze-answer-head` for the future hazard bridge. `--hazard-pretrain` intentionally raises `NotImplementedError` until that loop is implemented. 75: 76: ## Configuration 77: 78: | Config | Purpose | 79: |--------|---------| 80: | `configs/default.yaml` | Full runs with T5-large likelihood and 100k PPO timesteps | 81: | `configs/smoke.yaml` | Quick tests: 50 questions, TF-IDF likelihood, 3k PPO timesteps | 82: | `configs/t5_policy.yaml` | T5 policy pipeline: model, supervised, PPO, and data sections | 83: 84: qb-rl config aliases are supported (e.g., `data.dataset`, `likelihood.sbert_name`, `environment.reward` as alias for `reward_mode`). 85: 86: Additional environment options: 87: - `environment.end_mode: force_commit|no_buzz` controls horizon behavior 88: - `environment.no_buzz_reward` is only used when `end_mode: no_buzz` 89: 90: ## Compatibility Bridge 91: 92: Old qb-rl import paths that still resolve: 93: 94: - `qb_env.data_loader`, `qb_env.mc_builder`, `qb_env.text_utils` 95: - `models.answer_profiles` 96: - `agents.softmax_profile_buzzer` 97: 98: OpenAI support is opt-in only. Default local workflows stay offline-friendly and do not require the `openai` package or `OPENAI_API_KEY`. 99: 100: ## Conventions 101: 102: - NumPy-style docstrings with Parameters/Returns sections 103: - RL notation: `V` (value), `R` (reward), `T` (transition), `gamma` (discount), `s`/`a` (state/action) 104: - Prefer NumPy/PyTorch vectorized operations over loops in ML code 105: - Explicit seeds for reproducibility (use 1, 2, 3 for multi-seed runs) 1: # Quiz Bowl RL Buzzer (Unified) 2: 3: Unified CS234 final project codebase for quiz bowl buzzing under incremental clues. 4: 5: This repo keeps `qanta-buzzer` as the canonical implementation while preserving a qb-rl compatibility bridge: 6: 7: - Modular belief-feature pipeline: `qb_data/` -> `models/` -> `qb_env/` -> `agents/` -> `evaluation/` -> `scripts/` 8: - T5 policy pipeline: supervised warm-start and PPO for end-to-end text-based buzzing 9: - qb-rl-compatible import/config shims for older notebooks and scripts 10: - Optional OpenAI embedding support (`likelihood.model: openai`, `data.distractor_strategy: openai_profile`) 11: 12: ## Setup 13: 14: Requires Python >= 3.11. 15: 16: ```bash 17: python3 -m venv .venv 18: source .venv/bin/activate 19: pip install -U pip 20: pip install -e . 21: ``` 22: 23: Optional OpenAI support: 24: 25: ```bash 26: pip install -e '.[openai]' 27: export OPENAI_API_KEY=... 28: ``` 29: 30: ## Main Workflows 31: 32: ### Belief-feature / PPO pipeline 33: 34: The canonical four-stage smoke pipeline: 35: 36: ```bash 37: python scripts/build_mc_dataset.py --smoke 38: python scripts/run_baselines.py --smoke 39: python scripts/train_ppo.py --smoke 40: python scripts/evaluate_all.py --smoke 41: ``` 42: 43: `--smoke` selects `configs/smoke.yaml` and writes outputs to `artifacts/smoke/`. Drop `--smoke` for full runs (uses `configs/default.yaml`, writes to `artifacts/main/`). 44: 45: The smoke config uses tuned reward settings (`wait_penalty=0.05`, `early_buzz_penalty=0.2`, `ppo.seed=13`, `ppo.total_timesteps=3000`). 46: 47: `train_ppo.py` also accepts `--seed` to override the PPO/environment seed, and `--stochastic-eval` / `--deterministic-eval` to control post-training evaluation mode. 48: 49: ### T5 policy pipeline 50: 51: Trains a T5-based policy with supervised warm-start followed by PPO fine-tuning: 52: 53: ```bash 54: python scripts/train_t5_policy.py --config configs/t5_policy.yaml 55: python scripts/train_t5_policy.py --config configs/t5_policy.yaml --smoke # quick test with t5-small 56: ``` 57: 58: The T5 pipeline uses its own config (`configs/t5_policy.yaml`) which defines `model`, `supervised`, `ppo`, and `data` sections. It does not inherit `environment` or `likelihood` settings from the belief-feature configs -- the T5 PPO trainer uses default reward settings (`wait_penalty=0.1`). 59: 60: The T5 policy uses factorized action semantics: the wait head models `P(WAIT)` vs `P(BUZZ)`, the answer head models `P(answer | BUZZ)`, and the flat action distribution is `P(WAIT)` plus `P(BUZZ_i) = P(BUZZ) * P(answer_i | BUZZ)`. 61: 62: The CLI also reserves `--hazard-pretrain`, `--beta-terminal`, and `--freeze-answer-head` for an experimental hazard-style warm-start bridge. Those flags are parsed, but `--hazard-pretrain` currently raises `NotImplementedError` until the training loop is wired. 63: 64: ### Policy comparison 65: 66: ```bash 67: python scripts/compare_policies.py --t5-checkpoint checkpoints/ppo_t5/best_model 68: ``` 69: 70: Compares the MLP belief-feature policy against the T5 end-to-end policy on the same test set. Accuracy and buzz-position metrics are directly comparable. ECE and Brier are computed identically (top-answer probability at buzz time). S_q and reward comparisons are qualitative because the two architectures use different confidence semantics (belief-sigmoid vs wait-head probability) and different reward settings (config-driven vs T5-pipeline defaults). 71: 72: ### Additional scripts 73: 74: - `scripts/run_smoke_pipeline.py` -- runs all four smoke stages sequentially and writes a timing summary to `artifacts/smoke/smoke_pipeline_summary.json` 75: - `scripts/sweep_reward_shaping.py` -- grid sweep over `wait_penalty` and `early_buzz_penalty` with multi-seed evaluation 76: - `scripts/train_ppo.py --policy-mode flat_kplus1|stop_only` -- optional stop-only PPO surface; default remains `flat_kplus1` 77: - `generate_presentation.py` -- generates the Marp presentation slides 78: 79: ## Configuration 80: 81: Two primary YAML configs: 82: 83: | Config | Purpose | Key reward settings | 84: |--------|---------|-------------------| 85: | `configs/default.yaml` | Full runs | `wait_penalty=0.05`, `early_buzz_penalty=0.2`, `buzz_incorrect=-0.5` | 86: | `configs/smoke.yaml` | Quick tests (50 questions) | Same as default except `buzz_incorrect=-1.0`, `total_timesteps=3000` | 87: | `configs/t5_policy.yaml` | T5 pipeline | Own `model`/`supervised`/`ppo`/`data` sections; no `environment` | 88: 89: qb-rl config aliases are also supported: `data.dataset`, `data.dataset_config`, `likelihood.sbert_name`, `environment.reward` as an alias for `reward_mode`, etc. 90: 91: For horizon behavior, `environment.end_mode` defaults to `force_commit` (legacy behavior). Set `environment.end_mode: no_buzz` with `environment.no_buzz_reward` to end the episode without forcing a terminal answer. 92: 93: ## Testing 94: 95: 342 tests across 24 test files (3 skipped when optional extras not installed): 96: 97: ```bash 98: pytest # full suite 99: pytest tests/test_agents.py tests/test_environment.py tests/test_ppo_buzzer.py # quick iteration 100: ``` 101: 102: The test suite covers: 103: 104: - Baseline agents (threshold, softmax-profile, sequential Bayes) and PPO wrapper 105: - Gymnasium environment behavior, reward modes (including Expected Wins), and belief computation 106: - Likelihood model factories (TF-IDF, SBERT, DSPy with offline-safe stubs) 107: - T5 policy model, supervised trainer, and PPO trainer 108: - Evaluation metrics (S_q, Expected Wins, ECE, Brier score, calibration at buzz, per-category accuracy) 109: - Dataset split reproducibility (cross-process determinism) 110: - Variable-K dataset construction and mixed-K integration 111: - Opponent buzz models (logistic, empirical) 112: - qb-rl compatibility bridge 113: - Text observation wrapper 114: 115: ## Architecture 116: 117: ``` 118: qb_data/ Data loading, answer profiles, stratified splits, MC construction, DSPy profiles 119: qb_env/ Gymnasium environment, text wrapper, opponent models, optional StopOnlyEnv wrapper, qb-rl shims 120: models/ Likelihood models (TF-IDF, SBERT, T5, OpenAI, DSPy), belief features, T5 policy 121: agents/ Threshold, softmax-profile, sequential Bayes, PPO buzzer 122: evaluation/ S_q metric, Expected Wins, calibration, control experiments, plotting 123: scripts/ Pipeline entrypoints, DSPy compile, shared helpers 124: training/ T5 policy supervised + PPO trainers, hazard bridge utilities 125: configs/ YAML configuration files 126: artifacts/ Generated pipeline outputs (smoke/ and main/) 127: _legacy/ Pre-modularization prototypes (not installed) 128: ``` 129: 130: ## Compatibility Bridge 131: 132: These old qb-rl import paths resolve in this repo: 133: 134: - `qb_env.data_loader`, `qb_env.mc_builder`, `qb_env.text_utils` 135: - `models.answer_profiles` 136: - `agents.softmax_profile_buzzer` 137: 138: The bridge is additive. `qb_data/` remains the canonical home for data loading and MC construction. OpenAI support is opt-in only -- default local workflows stay offline-friendly. 139: 140: ## Documentation 141: 142: - `AGENTS.md` -- canonical repo contract for all coding agents (setup, architecture, testing, configuration) 143: - `CLAUDE.md` -- thin shim pointing to AGENTS.md with Claude-specific notes 144: - `walkthrough.md` -- end-to-end walkthrough exercising both pipelines (pre-remediation snapshot) 145: - `PRESENTATION.md` -- Marp presentation slides for the CS234 final project 146: - `.planning/` -- canonical project state, roadmap, architectural decisions, and remediation log 147: 148: ## Extensions (opt-in) 149: 150: Three opt-in extensions are available. All are disabled by default — the smoke pipeline and T5 smoke path work unchanged. 151: 152: ### Expected Wins reward mode 153: 154: Set `environment.reward_mode: expected_wins` and configure `environment.opponent_buzz_model` in YAML. Supports logistic and empirical (from `human_buzz_positions`) opponent models. Offline `expected_wins_score()` in `evaluation/metrics.py` uses the continuous formula: `V_self = g * R_correct + (1-g) * R_incorrect`. 155: 156: ### Variable-K answer choices 157: 158: Set `data.variable_K: true` and `data.min_K` / `data.max_K` in YAML. `MCBuilder` samples K per question. The env uses padded observations and `action_masks()`. Optional `MaskablePPO` via `pip install -e '.[maskable]'`. 159: 160: ### DSPy integration (experimental) 161: 162: Set `likelihood.model: dspy` and configure the `dspy` section in YAML. Requires `pip install -e '.[dspy]'`. Offline compile via `python scripts/optimize_dspy.py`. Does NOT integrate prompt optimization into PPO rollouts. 163: 164: ## Legacy Prototype 165: 166: The pre-modularization prototype (`main.py`, `environment.py`, `model.py`, `dataset.py`, `config.py`, etc.) has been moved to `_legacy/`. These files are not part of the installed package and are preserved only for reference. The modular `scripts/` pipeline above is the canonical workflow.
